messages.content:
A 50-year-old male sought a dental checkup and oral prophylaxis, noting a three-year interval since his last dentist visit. The patient, a daily smoker of 10 cigarettes, reported no other systemic diseases. Examination revealed painless, well-demarcated whitish plaques on the
buccal mucosa and both retro-commissures of the lips, which did not detach upon rasping. The patient was uncertain about the duration of these lesions.
The clinical features of the white lesions could be diagnosed as?

Pseudomembranous Candidiasis
Erythematous Candidiasis
Hyperplastic Candidiasis
Angular Cheilitis
Herpes simplex virus

The clinical features of the white lesions described could be diagnosed as Hyperplastic Candidiasis. This condition is commonly found in individuals with compromised immune systems, smoking, or poor oral hygiene. The painless, well-demarcated whitish plaques are characteristic of hyperplastic candidiasis.

A 55-year-old female patient with a history of type II diabetes mellitus presented to your clinic with a new-onset (3 months duration) solitary oral pigmented lesion measuring 1x1 cm on the lower lip. The lesion appears brown to dark brown in color, and its size has remained constant.

Perform a biopsy
Request a complete blood test
Follow up the case and do nothing
Take an X-ray to check for any amalgam remnants

In this case, it would be appropriate to perform a biopsy of the oral pigmented lesion. Since the patient has a history of type II diabetes mellitus and the lesion has been present for three months, it is important to rule out any potential malignant or premalignant conditions such as oral melanoma or oral squamous cell carcinoma. Biopsy will provide definitive diagnosis and guide further management.

Requesting a complete blood test may also be considered to evaluate the patient's overall health status, including blood sugar control, as diabetes can affect wound healing and increase the risk of infection. However, it is not a substitute for biopsy to diagnose the specific nature of the oral pigmented lesion.

Follow-up alone without further investigation or intervention would not be appropriate, as it is important to determine the nature of the lesion and rule out any potentially serious conditions.

Taking an X-ray to check for any amalgam remnants would not be relevant in this case, as the description of the lesion does not suggest any connection to dental amalgam or its remnants. The focus should be on investigating the pigmented lesion and ruling out oral malignancy.

A 21-year-old male patient presented at your clinic, and during the extra-oral examination, you noted fine, sparse hair, wrinkled, and hyperpigmented periocular skin. This patient is most likely experiencing:

Ectodermal dysplasia.
Xeroderma pigmentosum
Darier Disease
Peutz-Jeghers syndrome

Based on the clinical features described, the most likely condition that the 21-year-old male patient is experiencing is Xeroderma pigmentosum. Xeroderma pigmentosum is a rare genetic disorder characterized by extreme sensitivity to ultraviolet (UV) light, leading to increased risk of developing skin cancers. The fine, sparse hair, wrinkled, and hyperpigmented periocular skin are common features of this condition.

Ectodermal dysplasia is a genetic disorder that affects the development of ectodermal tissues, such as hair, teeth, nails, and sweat glands. It is not typically associated with the specific clinical features mentioned.

Darier Disease is an inherited condition characterized by skin abnormalities such as warty skin lesions and can also involve the nails. It does not typically present with the described periocular skin findings or hair abnormalities.

Peutz-Jeghers syndrome is a hereditary condition that causes the development of polyps in the gastrointestinal tract and is often associated with characteristic mucocutaneous pigmentation. However, it does not typically present with the specific periocular skin findings or hair abnormalities described in this case.

A 10-year-old boy, presenting with crusted lips and a history of fever, exhibits red blisters with
white centers on his face, hands, and feet. This boy most probably has:
*
Hand-foot and mouth disease
Erythema multiforme triggered by food
Erythema multiforme triggered by HPV infection
Erythema multiforme triggered by HSV infection

Based on the symptoms described, the most probable diagnosis for the 10-year-old boy is Hand-foot and mouth disease. Hand-foot and mouth disease is a common viral infection caused by the Coxsackievirus, characterized by red blisters with white centers on the face, hands, and feet. It is often accompanied by fever.

Erythema multiforme can present with similar skin lesions but is typically triggered by infections (such as HSV or Mycoplasma) or medications, rather than being associated with crusted lips and a history of fever triggered by food.

While erythema multiforme can be caused by HSV infection, the description of crusted lips and involvement of hands and feet is more consistent with Hand-foot and mouth disease. HPV infection is not associated with erythema multiforme.

Therefore, based on the symptoms provided, Hand-foot and mouth disease is the most likely diagnosis for the 10-year-old boy.

A 35-year-old female visited your clinic seeking restoration for her missing lower first molars, teeth #36 & #46. During history-taking, she mentioned a three-month complaint of psoriasis, treated with hydrocortisone for affected skin lesions. What oral manifestations would you expect to observe in her condition?
*
Fissure tongue
Erythema migrans
Hairy tongue
Scalloped tongue

In a patient with a history of psoriasis, the oral manifestations that could be observed include fissured tongue and erythema migrans. 

Fissured tongue is a common finding in patients with psoriasis. It is characterized by deep grooves or fissures on the dorsum of the tongue, giving it a wrinkled or furrowed appearance.

Erythema migrans, also known as geographic tongue, is another oral manifestation commonly seen in patients with psoriasis. It presents as irregular, red patches on the dorsum of the tongue, surrounded by a white or yellowish border. These patches may change in shape and location over time, giving the appearance of a "geographic" or map-like pattern.

Hairy tongue and scalloped tongue are not typically associated with psoriasis. Hairy tongue is caused by an overgrowth of the papillae on the tongue, giving it a hairy or furry appearance. Scalloped tongue is characterized by indentations or scalloping along the lateral borders of the tongue, which can be caused by pressure from the tongue against the teeth.

Therefore, in this particular case, one would expect to observe fissured tongue and erythema migrans as possible oral manifestations in a patient with a history of psoriasis.

A 45-year-old female patient with a history of hepatitis C, hypertension, elevated ALT & AST, and blood pressure measured at 135/105 mmHg, presented to the oral medicine clinic with extensive ulcerations, including the buccal mucosa and anterior tongue. Additionally, the lower labial mucosa exhibited white lacy lines with intact mucosa. The most probable diagnosis would
be:
*
Mucous membrane pemphigoid
Erosive lichen planus
Erythema Multiforme
Pemphigus Vulgaris

choose one

Based on the given clinical presentation, the most probable diagnosis for the 45-year-old female patient would be Mucous membrane pemphigoid.

Mucous membrane pemphigoid is a chronic autoimmune blistering disorder that primarily affects the mucous membranes, including the oral cavity. The extensive ulcerations on the buccal mucosa and anterior tongue are consistent with the erosive nature of mucous membrane pemphigoid. The lower labial mucosa exhibiting white lacy lines with intact mucosa, known as Wickham striae, is a characteristic finding in oral lichen planus, which is not the most probable diagnosis in this case.

Erosive lichen planus is another possible diagnosis that can present with ulcerations in the oral cavity. However, the presence of white lacy lines in the lower labial mucosa suggests mucous membrane pemphigoid as a more likely diagnosis.

Erythema multiforme typically presents with target-like lesions on the skin and mucous membranes, but it does not commonly cause extensive ulcerations in the oral cavity.

Pemphigus vulgaris is another autoimmune blistering disorder that can cause widespread mucosal blistering and erosions. However, the presence of intact mucosa with white lacy lines on the lower labial mucosa makes mucous membrane pemphigoid a more likely diagnosis than pemphigus vulgaris.

Therefore, based on the clinical presentation provided, the most probable diagnosis would be Mucous membrane pemphigoid.

A 42-year-old female patient attending the oral diagnosis course clinic exhibits claw-like fingers and a mouse face. Upon inquiry, she reveals that she has been diagnosed with:
*
Multiple sclerosis
Systemic lupus erythematosis
Systemic sclerosis
Tuberous sclerosis

coose one

Based on the clinical features described, the most probable diagnosis for the 42-year-old female patient would be Systemic sclerosis.

Systemic sclerosis, also known as scleroderma, is a chronic autoimmune connective tissue disease that affects the skin and various internal organs. The presence of claw-like fingers, which are characterized by tightening and thickening of the skin in the fingers, is a classic feature of systemic sclerosis. The term "mouse face" is often used to describe the facial changes associated with systemic sclerosis, including tight and thinning skin, narrowed lips, and a pinched appearance.

Multiple sclerosis is a neurological disorder that affects the central nervous system and does not typically cause the specific hand and facial changes described.

Systemic lupus erythematosus is another autoimmune disease that can affect multiple organs, but it does not typically present with claw-like fingers or mouse face.

Tuberous sclerosis is a genetic disorder characterized by the growth of benign tumors in multiple organs, including the brain, skin, kidneys, and lungs. It does not typically present with the specific hand and facial changes described.

Therefore, based on the clinical features provided, the most probable diagnosis for the 42-year-old female patient would be Systemic sclerosis.

A 55-year-old female patient, complaining of a toothache, reports a history of splenectomy due to splenomegaly five years ago. You order a complete blood count (CBC) to ensure everything is within normal limits. What would you expect to be elevated in the CBC report?
*
Red blood cells count
Platelets count
Neutrophils count
Eosinophils count

choose one

In a patient with a history of splenectomy, one would expect an elevated platelet count in the CBC report.

The spleen plays a role in regulating platelet levels in the bloodstream. After splenectomy, the body loses its ability to control platelet levels effectively, leading to an increase in platelet count. This condition is known as thrombocytosis, and it is a common finding in patients who have undergone splenectomy.

Elevated red blood cell count (polycythemia) is not typically associated with splenectomy. Neutrophil and eosinophil counts may vary depending on the patient's overall health status and any ongoing medical conditions but are not directly related to splenectomy. Thrombocytosis is the most likely finding in this particular case.

In diagnostic anesthesia methods used to localize a source of pain, which of the following conditions would you NOT anticipate a positive response (significant decrease in pain) to a local anesthetic (both injection and topical)?
*
Atypical odontalgia
Trigeminal neuralgia
Glossopharyngeal neuralgia
Traumatic neuroma

choose one

In the diagnostic anesthesia methods, the condition in which you would NOT anticipate a positive response to a local anesthetic (both injection and topical) is Trigeminal neuralgia.

Trigeminal neuralgia is a condition characterized by severe and recurrent facial pain. The pain is often triggered by mild stimulation of the trigeminal nerve branches. However, in Trigeminal neuralgia, the pain is typically not relieved by local anesthetic, including injection and topical application. The pain experienced in trigeminal neuralgia is due to damage or compression of the trigeminal nerve, and local anesthetic does not effectively block the transmission of pain signals in this situation.

On the other hand, Atypical odontalgia, Glossopharyngeal neuralgia, and Traumatic neuroma are conditions where a positive response (significant decrease in pain) to a local anesthetic is anticipated during diagnostic anesthesia methods.

A 55-year-old female patient, who underwent a kidney transplant three months ago, presents at your clinic with a large painful ulcer in the buccal mucosa. What is the best treatment for this ulcer?
*
Topical Lidocaine gel
Topical Corticosteroid
Intralesional Corticosteroid injection
Systemic Corticosteroid

choose one

In a patient who underwent a kidney transplant three months ago, presenting with a large painful ulcer in the buccal mucosa, the best treatment option for this ulcer would be Topical Corticosteroid.

Patients who have undergone organ transplantation are more susceptible to developing oral ulcerations and mucosal lesions due to the immunosuppressive medications they use to prevent organ rejection. Topical corticosteroids have anti-inflammatory properties and can help reduce pain and inflammation associated with oral ulcers in these patients. Topical corticosteroids, such as triamcinolone acetonide, can be applied directly to the ulcerated area to help promote healing and reduce symptoms.

Topical Lidocaine gel may help to reduce pain temporarily, but it does not directly address the underlying inflammation and promote healing.

Intralesional corticosteroid injection may be considered for large or refractory ulcers, but it is typically reserved for specific cases and not the first-line treatment.

Systemic corticosteroids may be prescribed in severe cases or when there are systemic manifestations, but they are not typically the first choice for a single painful oral ulcer localized to the buccal mucosa.

Therefore, the best treatment for the large painful ulcer in the buccal mucosa in a patient who underwent a kidney transplant three months ago would be Topical Corticosteroid.

An elderly male patient with a history of Bechet’s disease with long-term steroid use. He came to your clinic to report experiencing pain and swelling on the left side of his face, along
with a 2-week period of erythema on his skin. The pain aggravated during mealtime. An intraoral examination revealed pus discharge from the left Stenson's duct.
What diagnosis of the following in the top of the list?
*
Sjögren’s syndrome
Lymphoma
Bacterial sialadenitis
Mucoepidermoid carcinoma

choose one

Based on the symptoms described, the most probable diagnosis in this case would be Bacterial sialadenitis.

Bacterial sialadenitis refers to the inflammation and infection of the salivary glands, typically caused by bacterial organisms. The presence of pain and swelling on the left side of the face, along with pus discharge from the left Stenson's duct, are consistent with the diagnosis of sialadenitis.

Sjögren's syndrome is an autoimmune disorder characterized by the destruction of the exocrine glands, including the salivary glands. While it can cause dryness of the mouth and eyes, it is less likely to present with localized pain, swelling, and pus discharge from a specific duct.

Lymphoma and Mucoepidermoid carcinoma represent malignancies and would require further investigation, such as imaging and biopsy, to confirm the diagnosis. The symptoms described are more suggestive of an infectious and inflammatory process rather than a neoplastic condition.

Therefore, based on the symptoms provided, the most probable diagnosis in this case would be Bacterial sialadenitis.

Your clinic was visited by a 23-year-old ex-ballet dancer who reported a loud clicking sound on both sides of her TMJs without experiencing any discomfort. The opening of her mouth is 55mm, with a slight deviation to the right then to the left starting suddenly 16 months ago. Even after mandibular advancement, the examination revealed a clicking sound.
The most likely diagnosis in this case is? 
*
Disc displacement with reduction
Disc displacement without reduction
Hypermobility/subluxation of the TMJs
Dislocation of the TMJs

choose one

Based on the given clinical presentation, the most likely diagnosis in this case is Disc displacement with reduction.

Disc displacement with reduction refers to a condition where the articular disc of the temporomandibular joint (TMJ) temporarily moves out of its normal position and then returns to its original position with a clicking sound. The loud clicking sound reported by the patient without experiencing discomfort is consistent with this diagnosis.

Disc displacement without reduction occurs when the TMJ disc does not return to its normal position. This condition is typically associated with limited mouth opening and often causes pain and discomfort, which is not present in this case.

Hypermobility/subluxation of the TMJs refers to excessive movement of the TMJ beyond its normal range, which can cause instability and clicking sounds. However, the patient's symptoms of clicking sound with a slight deviation in mouth opening are more suggestive of disc displacement with reduction rather than hypermobility/subluxation.

Dislocation of the TMJs refers to a condition where the condyle completely moves out of the articular fossa and does not return to the normal position. This would usually cause significant pain and limited mouth opening. In this case, the mouth opening is measured at 55mm, which indicates that there is no complete dislocation of the TMJs.

Therefore, based on the given clinical presentation and findings, the most likely diagnosis is Disc displacement with reduction.

A 41-year-old female patient who was well-nourished came forward with a main complaint of painful and limited mouth opening. When she opens, her mouth moves towards the right side.
One week ago, she reported experiencing a trauma from her teenage son.
What is the most likely explanation for this case?
*
Disc displacement with reduction in the left TMJ
Chronic disc displacement without reduction in the right TMJ
Acute disc displacement without reduction in the right TMJ
Acute disc displacement without reduction in the left TMJ

choose one

Based on the given clinical presentation, the most likely explanation for this case is Acute disc displacement without reduction in the right TMJ.

Acute disc displacement without reduction occurs when the articular disc of the temporomandibular joint (TMJ) becomes dislodged and stays out of position, preventing it from returning to its normal anatomical position. This can result in painful and limited mouth opening, as well as deviations in jaw movement.

In this case, the patient's main complaint of painful and limited mouth opening, with the jaw moving towards the right side, suggests a unilateral TMJ issue. The recent trauma experienced by the patient
